evals.prompt: Devemos usar Vue 3 / Nuxt 4 ou Next.js para o novo portal de clientes da WizardingCode? A equipa conhece Vue mas o mercado PT parece estar a mover-se para React.
evals.expected_output: A requirements-anchored evaluation matrix scoring Vue/Nuxt 4 vs Next.js across all relevant dimensions, with Context7 used to fetch current docs, a scored comparison, and an ADR draft with a clear recommendation.
expectations: The skill calls kb_search and kb_ask for prior ADRs covering Vue/Nuxt/Next.js decisions and the 'Vue 3.6 / Nuxt 4 / Laravel 13 2026 ecosystem notes' already in the KB before evaluating.

The skill uses Context7 (mcp__context7__resolve-library-id → mcp__context7__query-docs) to retrieve current, version-specific documentation for both frameworks — it does not rely on training-data knowledge for API details.
Functional and non-functional requirements are anchored before scoring — team skills, existing stack compatibility, and time-to-productive are enumerated, not assumed.

The evaluation matrix is produced with at least 5 dimensions scored (1-5) with weights, including functional fit, ecosystem health, vendor/lock-in risk, team skill match, and one dimension specific to this context (e.g. community size in Portugal).
The ADR draft follows the required format (ADR-NNN, Context, Decision, Options considered, Consequences) and a kb_ingest call stores it with tags: adr, tech-decision, vue, next-js, eng.

evals.prompt: We're evaluating which LLM model to use for the lead-qualification agent: Claude Sonnet vs Claude Haiku vs GPT-4o. The agent runs 200 times per day, classifying leads against our ICP.
evals.expected_output: A token-economics-first evaluation with a cost matrix per model, a task-tier classification (Tier 1-2 for classification = Haiku first), a quality test recommendation, and an ADR draft with the cheapest model that meets quality bar.
expectations: The skill calls kb_search for prior model-selection decisions and the 'Harness Pattern Token Optimization' and token-economics fragments in the KB before evaluating.
The task is classified as Tier 1 (routing and classification) per the model selection matrix — leading to Haiku as the first recommendation, not Sonnet or Opus.
The token economics sub-matrix is produced with cost/1M tokens, context window, and caching support for each model — sourced from Context7 or KB, not from memory.
Monthly cost is estimated: 200 runs/day × 30 days × (input + output tokens estimated) per model — the 'cheapest model that meets quality bar' rule is applied explicitly.
The ADR recommends a quality test before committing to the tier, and flags that any tier change increasing cost on a high-frequency task requires the CEO balanced-decision gate.

evals.prompt: Precisamos de escolher uma base de dados para armazenar os vectores da KB. Estamos a comparar LanceDB (que já usamos), Pinecone e Qdrant. Mantemos o LanceDB ou migramos?
evals.expected_output: A KB-first evaluation that surfaces the existing LanceDB decision and context, a requirements-anchored matrix comparing LanceDB vs Pinecone vs Qdrant, and a recommendation to stay or migrate with a migration cost analysis if migration is proposed.
expectations: The skill calls kb_search for the existing KB architecture decision (LanceDB is documented in the project memory as part of the Graphify+LanceDB+Obsidian stack) — the prior decision must inform the evaluation.
Requirements are anchored first: data residency (GDPR/Portuguese), team familiarity with LanceDB, integration with existing Obsidian vault and Graphify, and cost per query at current KB scale.
The evaluation matrix scores all three candidates on functional fit, vendor lock-in risk, security posture, and cost — not just raw performance benchmarks.
If migration is proposed, the exit cost and migration complexity are quantified — the lock-in/exit-cost row is not skipped.
The ADR is drafted and ingested via kb_ingest; the recommendation names 'stay' or 'migrate' with the specific condition that would change the recommendation.
